fr:title.text: Path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

fr:title.text: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:title.text: Reflexive graphs (temporary section)
Reflexive graph
Displayed reflexive graph
Total reflexive graph

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

fr:title.text: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:title.text: Reflexive subgraph comprehension
Binary product of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:title.text: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

fr:title.text: Path object comprehension

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for types

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:title.text: Dependent product of contractible types

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: This follows immediately from univalence for functions.

fr:title.text: Dependent product of propositions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.

fr:title.text: Dependent product of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

fr:title.text: Binary product of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition ›

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition ›

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

fr:title.text: Embeddings are propositional ›

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.

fr:title.text: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherent remainder as a path object

fr:li.fr:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:

fr:title.text: Binary product of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: Path object comprehension

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:title.text: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:title.text: Dependent path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

fr:title.text: Displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:title.text: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

fr:title.text: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:title.text: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:title.text: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Path object structure on fibers

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

fr:title.text: Path object structure on sections
Path object structure on retractions
Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

fr:title.text: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Path object structure on retractions
Path object structure on sections
Dependent product of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

fr:title.text: Path object structure on fibers

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:title.text: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for types

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: Displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.

fr:title.text: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:title.text: Reflexive graph
Contractible type

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
